revision.chapter: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

sections.label: Revision Notes
sections.html:
Science is a systematic method to understand natural phenomena. It develops through curiosity, observation, experimentation, and reasoning. It is not just a collection of facts but a continuous process of building and refining knowledge about the world.

Scientific inquiry begins with curiosity and careful observation. Observing patterns in nature leads to questions, which further lead to investigation. Observation is the foundation on which scientific explanations and models are constructed.

Science progresses from simple observations to structured explanations. Observations are analyzed to identify patterns, which are then explained using models, theories, or laws supported by experimental evidence.

Models are simplified representations of real systems. They help in understanding complex phenomena by focusing only on important features while ignoring unnecessary details that do not affect the main outcome.

Simplification is essential in science because real-world systems are highly complex. By ignoring less relevant factors, scientists can focus on key variables and make meaningful predictions and explanations.

Every scientific model involves assumptions. These assumptions are intentional simplifications used to make problems manageable. They are not errors but necessary steps to study systems effectively and systematically.

Science uses precise and standardized language to avoid confusion. Words in science often have specific meanings that differ from everyday usage. This ensures clarity and uniform understanding across different regions.

Scientific quantities are represented using symbols like m, v, F, and I. Standard units are used globally to ensure consistency in measurement and communication of scientific data and results.

Mathematics is used as a language in science to express relationships between quantities. Equations help in analyzing systems, making predictions, and understanding how different variables are connected.

Scientific equations represent relationships between physical quantities rather than just calculations. They describe how one quantity changes with respect to another and help in predicting real-world behavior.

Standard units like the SI system are used worldwide to avoid confusion. Uniform measurement systems ensure accuracy, consistency, and fairness in science, trade, and daily life applications.

Laws describe consistent natural patterns, theories explain why those patterns occur, and principles provide guiding frameworks. All are based on evidence and are subject to revision with new discoveries.

Scientific knowledge is not fixed. It evolves over time as new evidence is discovered. Old ideas may be modified or replaced, making science a dynamic and self-correcting system.

Science allows predictions based on established laws and models. These predictions are tested through experiments. Agreement between prediction and observation strengthens scientific understanding.

Estimation is an important scientific skill used to check whether answers are reasonable. Science is interdisciplinary, combining physics, chemistry, biology, and mathematics to solve real-world problems. Scientific thinking emphasizes reasoning, questioning, evidence-based conclusions, and critical evaluation of information rather than memorization.

revision.chapter: Cell: The Building Block of Life

sections.label: How to Study Cells?
sections.html:
Human eye can distinguish two points only if they are at least 0.1 mm apart. Cells are usually smaller than this limit, making unaided observation impossible.

Cells are too small for direct vision, so microscopes are required. They use lenses to magnify objects and make cellular structures visible for scientific study and analysis.

Light microscopes use objective and eyepiece lenses (10X, 40X etc.) to magnify objects. They help in observing cell structure using visible light and provide moderately clear images.

Robert Hooke first observed cells in 1665 using a microscope. He studied cork and saw box-like compartments, which he named “cells,” marking the beginning of cell biology.

Cell size can be estimated using microscope field diameter and number of cells observed across it. This helps calculate approximate actual size of microscopic structures.

Electron microscopes use electron beams instead of light. They provide very high magnification and resolution, allowing observation of cell structures at nanometre scale.

Improved microscopes increase magnification, resolution, and contrast. These features help scientists observe finer details of cells and understand their structure more accurately.

sections.label: Structure of a Cell
sections.html:
Cell membrane is a thin, selectively permeable boundary. It controls movement of substances between cell and environment and maintains internal balance of the cell.

Osmosis is movement of water through a membrane from dilute to concentrated solution. Diffusion is movement of particles from higher to lower concentration.

Plant, fungi, and bacterial cells have a cell wall outside the membrane. It provides rigidity, protection, and structural support, helping plants maintain shape and withstand environmental conditions.

Plant cells maintain shape due to rigid wall, even when water is lost. Animal cells shrink in concentrated solutions as they lack a cell wall.

Cell membrane consists of lipid bilayer with embedded proteins. Molecules move freely, making membrane fluid. Proteins act as gatekeepers for transport of substances.

Plant cells are regularly shaped due to cell wall, while animal cells are irregular due to absence of rigid outer covering, allowing flexibility in movement.

Cell wall is mainly made of cellulose in plants. It provides strength and is permeable, allowing water and minerals to pass while maintaining structural integrity.

The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

sections.label: The Cell Interior - A Coordinated Working System
sections.html:
Cell consists of plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus. Cytoplasm contains organelles responsible for different life processes and biochemical activities inside the cell.

Prokaryotic cells lack a true nucleus and membrane-bound organelles. Eukaryotic cells have a well-defined nucleus and specialized organelles for complex cellular functions.

Nucleus has nuclear membrane, nucleolus, and chromatin. It controls cell activities and contains genetic material (DNA) responsible for inheritance of traits.

Ribosomes are small structures found in cytoplasm or attached to Endoplasmic Reticululm (ER). They are responsible for protein synthesis required for cell functioning and growth.

Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER) forms a network in cytoplasm. Rough ER helps in protein synthesis, while smooth ER helps in lipid and hormone synthesis and storage.

Golgi apparatus modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids. It works like a cell’s processing and shipping center for transport and secretion.

Mitochondria produce energy in form of ATP. Plastids in plants help in food synthesis and storage. Both have their own DNA and ribosomes.

Prokaryotic cells do not have a well-defined nucleus, instead their genetic material is present in a region called the nucleoid. They also lack membrane-bound organelles.

Eukaryotic cells are larger and more complex. They have a welldefined nucleus and several membrane-bound organelles.

sections.label: How do Normal Cells Grow and Divide?
sections.html:
Cells divide to produce new cells for growth, repair, and reproduction. Growth of organisms occurs due to increase in cell number, not cell size alone.

Onion root tip cells show active division. Different stages are visible because cells continuously divide, showing structural variations during the process.

Eukaryotic cells divide in an orderly manner called the cell cycle. It ensures controlled growth and proper distribution of genetic material.

Mitosis produces two identical daughter cells from one parent cell. Each daughter cell receives the same genetic material and chromosome number as the parent.

Meiosis produces four gametes with half chromosome number. It occurs in reproductive cells and introduces genetic variation in offspring.

Meiosis occurs in reproductive organs to form sperm and egg cells. It ensures genetic diversity and restoration of chromosome number during fertilisation.

Cell culture is a method of growing cells outside the body in controlled conditions. It is used for research, medicine, and biochemical production.

All cells are surrounded by a cell membrane. In addition, cells of plants, fungi and bacteria have a cell wall outside the cell membrane.

The nucleus of eukaryotic cells contains chromosomes, which are composed of DNA and associated proteins, which carry genetic information.

Plant cells also contain special organelles called plastids, such as chloroplasts, leucoplasts and chromoplasts.

Mitosis produces two daughter cells identical to the parent cell.

Meiosis is a two-step division process which produces four daughter cells, each having half the number of chromosomes.

Normal cells grow in a controlled manner, perform their functions and die naturally, while cancer cells lose control and keep dividing uncontrollably leading to the formation of tumours.

sections.label: Cell Theory — The Unifying Principle of Biology
sections.html:
Schleiden stated plants are made of cells, Schwann stated animals are made of cells, and Virchow stated cells arise from pre-existing cells.

Cell theory states that all organisms are made of cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, and all cells come from existing cells.

Cell performs all essential life functions. All organisms, from bacteria to humans, are made of cells that work as structural and functional units.

New cells arise from existing cells through division. This ensures continuity of life and transfer of genetic information across generations.

Normal animal cells stop dividing when they touch each other. This is called contact inhibition, which helps regulate growth and maintain tissue structure.

Cancer cells lose control of division and grow uncontrollably. This leads to tumour formation and disruption of normal body functions.

Cells are programmed to die when no longer needed. This maintains balance in the body and supports proper development and functioning of tissues.

All cells are filled with cytoplasm. In eukaryotic cells, cytoplasm contains  several  cell  organelles,  each  performing  a  specific  function.

Important cell organelles include the nucleus, endoplasmic reticulum, mitochondria, golgi apparatus, ribosomes and lysosomes.

sections.label: Are Plants and Animals Made of Same Types of Tissues
sections.html:
Examples of tissue:

  • In human beings the cells of the muscular tissue contract and relax and help in movements.
  • In plants the cells of the vascular tissue conduct water and food from one part of the plant to the other.

Tissue is a group of cells having similar structure and function. In plants and animals groups of cells called tissues carry out specific functions.

Differences between plant and animal tissues:-

  1. Plant tissues :-
    1. Plants do not move from place to place. Most of the cells and tissues in plants provide mechanical support and strength. So most of the tissues consist of dead cells because they provide mechanical support and strength to the plant.
    2. The growth of plants takes place only in some regions. So plants have tissues called meristematic tissues which divide and help in growth and permanent tissues which do not divide.
  2. Animal tissues :-
    1. Animals move from place to place and need more energy than plants. So most cells and tissues in animals are living cells.
    2. The growth of animals is more uniform so animals do not have separate dividing and non-dividing cells. Animals have organs having specialized functions. So the organs have specialized tissues.

sections.label: Plant Tissues
sections.html:
Xylem: consists of tracheids, vessels, and xylem parenchyma and xylem fibres. The tracheids and vessels help to transport water and minerals from roots to all part of the plant. Xylem parenchyma stores food and fibers help in support.

Complex permanent tissues: Complex tissues are made of more than one type of cells. All these cells coordinate to perform a common function. Xylem and phloem are examples of such complex tissues.

Epidermis: Epidermis is the outer protective covering of the plant and is usually layered by cuticle. The epidermis is usually made of a single layer of cells. In some plants living in very dry habitats, the epidermis may be thicker avoid critical water loss.

Permanent Tissues: When the cells take up a specific role and lose the ability to divide they form a permanent tissue. This process of taking up a permanent shape, size, and a function is called differentiation. Permanent tissues are of two types - simple permanent tissues and complex permanent tissues.

Meristematic tissues are growth tissues and found in the growing regions of the plant. According to their position in plant, meristems are apical, lateral and intercalary.

Characteristics of Meristematic Tissues:

  • Their cells are similar in structure and have thin cellulose cell walls.
  • The cells are active and have dense cytoplasm.
  • They contain few vacuoles or no vacuoles at all.
  • The meristematic cells are compactly arranged and do not contain any intercellular space between them.

Simple permanent tissues are composed of cells which are structurally and functionally similar. They are of three types - parenchyma, collenchyma and sclerenchyma.

  • Parenchyma tissue consists of relatively unspecialised cells with thin cell walls. They are live cells.
  • The cells of collenchyma tissue are living, elongated and irregularly thickened at the corners.
  • The cells of sclerenchyma tissue are dead. They are long and narrow as the walls are thickened due to deposition of lignin.

Phloem: - consists of sieve tubes, companion cells, and phloem parenchyma and phloem fibres. The sieve tubes and companion cells transports food from leaves to all parts of the plant. Phloem parenchyma stores food and fibres  help in support.

sections.label: Animal Tissues
sections.html:
Structure of Neuron

Neuron (Nerve cell): has a cell body containing a nucleus and cytoplasm. It has hair like structures called dendrites and a long part called axon having nerve endings. The axon has a protective covering called myelin sheath. The neurons are joined together to form nerve fibres. The junction between two neurons is called synapse.

Striated muscles: are voluntary muscles having light and dark striations. The cells are long, cylindrical and are multinucleated.  They are also called skeletal muscles because they are attached to bones. They help in voluntary movements of the body.

Areolar connective tissue – It is the simplest and most widely distributed connective tissue. It is found between the skin and muscles, around blood vessels and nerves and in the bone marrow. It fills the space inside the organs, supports internal organs and helps in repair of tissues.

Bone Cartilage
Hard and inflexible Flexible
Porous Non-porous
Blood vessels present Blood vessels absent
Matrix made up of protein and mineral sells (e.g., calcium phosphate) Matrix made up of protein
Tendons Ligaments
Inelastic Elastic
Join muscles to bone. Connect bones to bones.
Made up of white collagen fibres. Made up of white collagen as well as yellow elastin fibres.

Cartilage: Cartilage is specialized connective tissue which is compact and less vascular. It has widely spaced out cells. Its matrix is composed of proteins and sugars, and is slightly hardened by calcium salts. Cartilage smoothens bone surfaces at joints and is also present in the nose, ear, trachea and larynx.

Tendons: Tendons are cord-like, strong, inelastic structures that join muscles to bones. They have limited flexibility.

Ligaments: These are elastic structures which connect bone to bone. It has great strength but contains very little matrix.

Fluid connective tissue (Blood): Blood is a fluid connective tissue. It is composed of plasma, platelets, red blood cells and white blood cells.

Glandular epithelial tissue: - Sometimes epithelial tissue folds inwards and forms a multicellular gland which secretes substances. This tissue is called  Glandular epithelial.

Cuboidal epithelial tissue: - Consists of cone shape cells having a basement membrane. It forms the lining of kidney, tubules and ducts of salivary gland sand provide mechanical support.

Animal tissues are of four main types. They are: - Epithelial tissue, Muscular  tissue, Connective tissue and Nervous tissue.

  • Epithelial tissues:- Are of different types. They are: - Squamous, Columnar, Cuboidal, Glandular etc.
  • Muscular tissues:- Are of three types. They are :- Striated, Unstriated and  Cardiac.
  • Connective tissues:- Are of different types. They are :- Blood, Bone,  Ligaments, Tendons, Cartilage, Areolar, Adipose etc.
  • Nervous tissue:- Consists of nerve cells called neurons.

Functions of epithelial tissues:

  • They form the outer lining of the skin and protect the underlying cells from drying, injury and chemical effects.
  • They form the lining of mouth and alimentary canal and protect these organs.
  • They help in absorption of water and nutrients.
  • They help in elimination of waste products.
  • They form secretory function by secreting a variety of substances such as sweat, saliva, enzymes, etc.

Depending upon the shape and function of the cells, the epithelial tissues are classified as:

  1. Squamous epithelium
  2. Cuboidal epithelium
  3. Columnar epithelium
  4. Ciliated epithelium
  5. Glandular epithelium

Squamous epithelial tissue- consists of a layer of thin flat cells. It is present in the linings of blood vessels, lungs, mouth, oesophagus etc. The skin has several layers of epithelial cells. This tissue is called Stratified squamous tissue.

Columnar epithelial tissue: consists of tall pillar like cells. It is present in the walls of the intestine. The columnar epithelial tissue in the respiratory tract has hair like projections called cilia. This tissue is called ciliated columnar epithelial tissue.

Bone: - is a hard and strong tissue. It consists of cells which are embedded in a hard matrix containing calcium and phosphorus compounds. Bone forms the framework of the body and supports the body and the main organs.

Adipose connective tissue: It is basically an aggregation of fat cells called adipocytes. It is found below the skin and between internal organs. It keeps the visceral organs in position and forms shock-absorbing cushions around kidneys and eye balls.

Muscular tissue: Muscular tissue helps in the movements of the body. It consists of elongated cells called muscle fibres. It contains a protein called contractile protein which contracts and relaxes and helps in movements. Muscles which can move under our will are called voluntary muscles. Muscles which do not move under our will are called involuntary muscles.

Unstriated muscles (Smooth muscles): are involuntary muscles  having no striations. The cells are long and spindle shaped and are uninucleated. They are present in alimentary canal, blood vessels, bronchi of lungs, iris of eye etc. They help in involuntary movements.

Cardiac muscles: - are involuntary muscles having faint striations.  The cells are long, cylindrical, branched and multinucleated. They are present in the heart and helps in the contraction and relaxation of the heart.

Striated Non-striated Cardiac
They are present in the limbs, body walls, tongue, pharynx and beginning of oesophagus. They are present in the oesophagus (posterior part only). urino-gential tract, urinary bladder, vessels, iris of eye, dermis of skin, and arrector pili muscles of hair. They are present in the wall of the heart, pulmonary veins and superior vena cava.
Cylindrical Spindle shaped. Cylindrical.
Fibres unbranched. Fibres unbranched Fibres branched.
Multinuclecate. Uninucleate Uninucleate.
Bounded by sarocolemma. Bounded by plasmalemma. Bounded by sarcolemmi
Light and dark bands present Light and dark bands absent Faint light and dark bands present.

The brain, spinal cord and nerves are all composed of the nervous tissue. The cells of this tissue are called nerve cells or neurons.

The neuron consists of 3 parts:

  • The Cyton or Cell body - It contains a central nucleus and cytoplasm with deeply stained particles called Nissl’s granules.
  • Dendrites - The dendrons are short processes arising from the cyton and branches into dendrites.
  • Axon - It is a single, long cylindrical process which forms fine branches terminally. It has a swollen structure at its end called synaptic knob or bouton. It is also termed as the nerve fibre.

sections.label: Why are Plant and Animal Tissues Different?
sections.html:
Plant tissues contain rigid cell walls providing strength and support, while animal tissues lack cell walls, making them flexible. This structural difference enables plants to remain stationary and animals to move freely.

Plants are generally immobile and require supportive tissues, whereas animals move actively. Therefore, animal tissues are specialized for movement, coordination, and flexibility, unlike plant tissues which focus more on support and growth.

Plants prepare their own food through photosynthesis using specialized tissues, while animals depend on external food sources and possess tissues for digestion, absorption, and transport of nutrients.

Plant growth is continuous and occurs at specific regions like meristems, whereas animal growth is limited and stops after maturity. This difference leads to distinct tissue organization and functions in both organisms.

Plants rely on tissues like sclerenchyma and collenchyma for mechanical support, while animals depend on bones and connective tissues, providing both support and mobility.

Plants use xylem and phloem for transport of water and food, while animals have complex circulatory systems involving blood and vessels for efficient distribution of substances.

In plants, tissues mainly support growth and transport, while in animals, tissues are highly specialized for diverse functions like movement, sensation, and coordination, reflecting higher complexity.

Tissues are groups of similar cells that work together to perform specific functions.

Different tissues coordinate with one another to perform life processes in plants and animals.

sections.label: Tissues for Growth in Plants
sections.html:
Meristematic tissues consist of actively dividing cells responsible for plant growth. These cells are small, have dense cytoplasm, and continuously divide to form new cells, contributing to overall development.

Apical meristem is present at root and shoot tips and is responsible for increasing plant length. It helps roots grow deeper and shoots grow taller by continuous cell division.

Lateral meristem is found along the sides of stems and roots and increases girth or thickness. It produces new cells inward and outward, forming annual rings and strengthening the plant.

Intercalary meristem is located at nodes or internodes and helps plants regrow after cutting or grazing. It is commonly found in grasses and enables rapid regeneration and branching.

Differentiation is the process by which meristematic cells lose their ability to divide and become specialized permanent tissues, performing specific functions like support, storage, or transport.

Permanent tissues are formed after differentiation and consist of cells that no longer divide. They are specialized for functions such as protection, support, and conduction in plants.

Permanent tissues are classified into simple (parenchyma, collenchyma, sclerenchyma) and complex (xylem, phloem). They perform roles like storage, support, flexibility, and transport of substances.

Plant tissues are broadly classified into meristematic and permanent tissues, depending on their ability to divide.

Functionally, plant tissues can be categorised as protecting tissue, supporting tissue and conducting tissue.

Permanent tissues may be simple (made-up of one type of cell) or complex (made-up of more than one type of cell).

Simple permanent tissues include parenchyma, collenchyma and schlerenchyma.

Complex permanent tissues include xylem and phloem that transport water and food respectively to all parts of the plant.

sections.label: Animal Tissues
sections.html:
Epithelial tissue covers body surfaces and lines internal organs. It protects against injury, controls substance exchange, and performs functions like absorption, secretion, and filtration.

Connective tissue supports and connects different body parts. It includes blood, bone, cartilage, tendons, and ligaments, each having a matrix that determines its structure and function.

 

Blood transports oxygen, nutrients, and wastes. It contains RBCs, WBCs, platelets, and plasma, playing roles in immunity, clotting, and maintaining internal balance.

Muscular tissue enables movement through contraction. It includes skeletal muscles for voluntary actions, smooth muscles for involuntary actions, and cardiac muscles for continuous heartbeats.

Nervous tissue controls and coordinates body functions. It consists of neurons that transmit signals between the brain, spinal cord, and other body parts, enabling quick responses.

Muscles are of three types—skeletal (voluntary), smooth (involuntary), and cardiac (heart muscles). Each type is structurally adapted for specific movements and functions.

Animal tissues show high specialization, allowing efficient performance of complex functions such as movement, coordination, protection, and communication within the body.

Animal tissues are mainly of four types; epithelial, connective, muscular and nervous tissues.

Epithelial tissue forms the outer covering of the body or protective lining to internal organs to provide protection and other functions.

Connective tissue connects and supports various organs and tissues of the body.

Muscular tissue produces voluntary and involuntary movements for locomotion, and other movements in the body.

Nervous tissue consists of neurons to receive and transmit impulses, and helps regulate body activities.

sections.label: The Musculoskeletal System
sections.html:
The musculoskeletal system consists of bones, muscles, joints, cartilage, tendons, and ligaments, working together to provide structure, movement, and protection to the body.

Bones provide support, shape, and protection to vital organs. They form the framework of the body and act as levers for movement when muscles contract.

Muscles contract and relax to produce movement. They work in pairs and are attached to bones via tendons, enabling voluntary and involuntary movements.

Joints connect bones and allow movement. Different types of joints permit different kinds of movements such as bending, rotation, or limited motion.

Tendons connect muscles to bones, while ligaments connect bones to each other. They provide strength, stability, and flexibility to joints.

The musculoskeletal system works under the control of the nervous system. Signals from the brain coordinate muscle movements, ensuring smooth and controlled actions.

This system helps in posture, locomotion, and protection of organs. It also enables everyday activities like walking, lifting, and maintaining balance.

Skeletal system protects organs and provides support.

In our body, movement occurs by the coordination of muscles and bones (musculoskeletal system), under the control of the nervous system.

sections.label: Types of Joints
sections.html:
This joint allows movement in all directions. Found in shoulders and hips, it enables rotation and circular movement due to the ball-shaped end fitting into a socket.

Hinge joints allow movement in one direction, like bending and straightening. Examples include the elbow and knee, functioning like a door hinge.

Pivot joints allow rotational movement around a central axis. The neck joint enables side-to-side movement of the head.

Different joints allow varying degrees of movement, enabling the body to perform complex actions ranging from simple bending to full rotation.

Joints provide flexibility and mobility to the body. Without joints, movement would not be possible, making them essential for daily activities.

Joints are supported by ligaments and cartilage, which protect bones from damage and ensure stability during movement.

sections.label: Skeletal System
sections.html:
The skeletal system is made up of bones forming a framework that supports the body, maintains shape, and protects internal organs.

The skull protects the brain and sensory organs. It consists of flat bones joined by fixed joints, ensuring safety and stability.

The backbone supports the body and protects the spinal cord. It is flexible due to cartilage discs between vertebrae, allowing bending and twisting.

The rib cage protects the heart and lungs. It expands and contracts during breathing due to flexible cartilage connections.

Bones contain minerals like calcium and phosphorus, making them strong and rigid. They also store minerals and produce blood cells in bone marrow.

Bones act as levers for muscles. When muscles contract, bones move at joints, enabling body movements.

Proper nutrition, exercise, and posture are essential to maintain strong bones and a healthy skeletal system, preventing injuries and disorders.

sections.label: Describing Motion
sections.html:
Uniform motion :-If a body travels equal distances in equal intervals of time, it is said to be in uniform motion.

Displacement is the length of the shortest path travelled by a body from its initial position to its final position.

Distance moved is the actual length of the path travelled by a body.

Motion is the change in position of a body with time. Motion can be described in terms of the distance moved or the displacement.

A body is said to be in a state of rest when its position does not change with respect to a reference point.

Let A, B and C represents the position of the object at different instants. First the object moves through C and B and reaches A. Then it moves back along the same path and reaches C through B.
The total path length covered by the object is OA + AC, that is 60 km + 35 km = 95 km.

Difference between Distance and Displacement

Distance Displacement
Length of the actual path travelled by an object. Shortest length between the initial point and far point of an object.
It is a scalar quantity. It is a vector quantity.
It remains positive, can't be '0' or negative. It can be positive (+ve), negative (-ve), or zero.
The distance can be equal to displacement (in linear path). Displacement can be equal to the distance in the linear path or it is lesser than distance.

Non uniform motion :- If a body travels unequal distances in equal intervals of time, it is said to be in non uniform motion.

  • Continuous increase in slope of curve indicates accelerated non-uniform motion.
  • Continuous decrease in slope of curve indicates decelerate non-uniform motion.

Non-uniform motion is of two types:

  • Accelerated motion: When motion of body increase with unequal time.
  • De-accelerated motion or Non uniform retardation: When motion of a body decreases with unequal interval of time.

sections.label: Measuring The Rate of Motion
sections.html:
SI unit of velocity is m/s. Velocity can be positive(+ve), negative(-ve) or zero.

Average velocity is the mean of the initial velocity u and final velocity v.

Average velocity is the ratio of the total displacement to the total time taken.
{tex}\text {Average velocity }=\frac{\text { Total displacement }}{\text { Total time }}{/tex}

Velocity is the speed of a body in given direction. It is vector quantity.
{tex}\text {Velocity }=\frac{\text { Displacement }}{\text { Time }}{/tex}

Average speed is the ratio of the total distance travelled to the total time taken.
{tex}\text {Average speed }=\frac{\text { Total distance travelled }}{\text { Total time taken }}{/tex}

If a body is travelling with non-uniform motion, then the speed will not remain uniform but have different values throughout the motion of such body.

If a body is executing uniform motion, then there will be a constant speed or uniform motion.

The SI unit of speed is metre per second. This is represented by the symbol ms-1 or m/s.

The measurement of distance travelled by a body per unit time is called speed. It is denoted by v.
{tex}Speed =\frac{\text { Distance travelled }}{\text { Time taken }} {/tex}
{tex}V=\frac{s}{t} {/tex}

sections.label: Rate of Change of Velocity
sections.html:
Non uniform acceleration: If the change in velocity is unequal in equal intervals of time it is non uniform acceleration.

Uniform acceleration: If the change in velocity is equal in equal intervals of time it is uniform acceleration

Retardation/Deacceleration: Deacceleration is seen in uniform motion during decrease in velocity with time.

The SI unit of acceleration is m/s2. Acceleration is a vector quantity as it has direction and magnitude both.

Acceleration is a measure of the change in the velocity of an object per unit time.
Acceleration {tex} =\frac{\text { Change in velocity }}{\text { Time }}{/tex}
Acceleration {tex} =\frac{\text { Final Velocity Initial Velocity }}{\text { Time }}{/tex}
{tex}a=\frac{v-u}{t}{/tex}
where, v = final velocity, u initial velocity
If v > u, then 'a' will be positive (+ve).

sections.label: Graphical Representation of Motion
sections.html:
When a body is moving with uniform retardation and its initial velocity is not zero. As  θ > 90°, graph has a negative slope.

When a body is moving with decreasing acceleration. Slope decreases with time.

When body is moving with increasing acceleration. Slope increases with time.

When a body starts from rest and moves with uniform acceleration. Greater is the slope of v-t graph, greater will be the acceleration.

When a body moving with uniform velocity , the slope of AB indicates zero acceleration.

Distance - Time Graphs: For body moving with uniform speed.

Distance - Time Graphs: For a body at rest , as the slope is zero, so speed of the body is zero.

Distance - Time Graphs: The change in the position of an object with time can be represented on the distance-time graph. In this graph, time is taken along the x-axis and distance is taken along the y-axis.

Distance - Time Graphs: For accelerated motion, The slope of graph is increasing with time.

Distance - Time Graphs:  For decelerated motion, Slope of graph is decreasing with time.

Velocity- Time Graphs: The variation in velocity with time for an object moving in a straight line can be represented by a velocity-time graph. In this graph, time is represented along the x-axis and the velocity is represented along the y-axis.

= u {tex}\times{/tex} t + {tex}\frac1 2{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} t {tex}\times{/tex} (v - u)
{tex}\left(\because \frac{v-u}{t}=a\right){/tex} so [v - u = at]
= ut + {tex}\frac12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} t {tex}\times{/tex} at
{tex}s=\frac{(u+v) \times t}{2}{/tex}
Or {tex}s=\left(\frac{u+v}{2}\right) \times\left(\frac{v-u}{a}\right) \left(\because \frac{v-u}{t}=a\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore s=\frac{v^{2}-u^{2}}{2 a}{/tex}
Or v2 = u2 + 2as

sections.label: Uniform Circular Motion
sections.html:
Velocity of an object in a circular motion is: {tex}\mathrm{V}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}{\mathrm{t}}{/tex}

The motion of a body in a circular path is called circular motion.

If a body moves in a circular path with uniform speed, its motion is called uniform circular motion. Uniform circular motion is accelerated motion because in a circular motion a body continuously changes its direction.

sections.label: Motion in a Straight Line
sections.html:
Motion:
Motion occurs when an object changes its position with time relative to a reference point. If position remains constant, the object is at rest. Motion is always described with respect to a chosen reference.

Reference Point and Position:
To describe an object's position, a fixed point called the origin is chosen. Position is a vector quantity that specifies both the distance and the direction of the object from this origin, often using plus (+) and minus (–) signs for straight-line motion.

Distance and Displacement:
Distance is a scalar quantity representing the total path length covered by an object. Displacement is a vector quantity representing the shortest straight-line distance from the initial to the final position, including direction.

Uniform and Non- Uniform:
An object is in uniform motion if it travels equal distances in equal intervals of time. It is in non-uniform motion if it travels unequal distances in equal time intervals, indicating a change in speed or direction.

Average Speed:
Average speed is a scalar quantity calculated as the total distance travelled divided by the total time interval taken.

{tex}\text{Average Speed} = \frac{\text{Total Distance}}{\text{Total Time}}{/tex}

Average Velocity:
Average velocity is a vector quantity defined as the net displacement divided by the total time interval.

{tex}v_{av} = \frac{s}{t}{/tex}

Average Acceleration:
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity over time. It is calculated by dividing the change in velocity (final velocity {tex}v{/tex} minus initial velocity {tex}u{/tex}) by the time interval ({tex}t{/tex}).

{tex}a = \frac{v - u}{t}{/tex}

The distance and direction of an object with respect to the reference point, at any instant of time, describes the position of the object at that instant of time.

If the position of the object with respect to a reference point changes with time, the object is said to be in motion.

sections.label: Graphical Representation of Motion
sections.html:
Importance of Graphs:
Graphs provide a systematic way to represent motion by showing how one physical quantity changes with another. They help in analysing trends, comparing motions, and identifying whether motion is uniform or accelerated.

Plotting a Graph:
A graph is plotted by taking time on the horizontal axis (x-axis) and another physical quantity like position or velocity on the vertical axis (y-axis). Proper choice of scale and accurate plotting of data points are necessary for correct representation.

Position-Time Graph:
The nature of motion is identified from the shape of a position-time graph. A straight line represents uniform motion with constant velocity. A curved line indicates changing velocity, meaning accelerated motion. A horizontal line shows that position does not change, so the object is at rest.

Velocity from Position-Time Graph:
Velocity is determined from the slope of the position-time graph. The slope represents how quickly position changes with time. A steeper slope indicates higher velocity, while a less steep slope indicates lower velocity.

Velocity-Time Graph:

The shape of a velocity-time graph indicates the nature of motion. A horizontal line represents constant velocity and zero acceleration. A straight line with a positive slope indicates uniform acceleration, while a negative slope represents uniform deceleration.

Acceleration from Velocity-Time Graph
Acceleration is obtained from the slope of the velocity-time graph. It represents the rate of change of velocity with time. A positive slope indicates increasing velocity, a negative slope indicates decreasing velocity, and zero slope means constant velocity.

Displacement from Velocity-Time Graph
Displacement is determined from the area under the velocity-time graph over a given time interval. This area represents the total change in position of the object during that time.

Displacement is the net change in the position of the object between two given instants of time.

sections.label: Kinematic Equations for Motion in a Straight Line with Constant Acceleration
sections.html:
Concept of Constant Acceleration: When an object's velocity changes by equal amounts in equal time intervals, it is in uniformly accelerated motion, which can be described using specific mathematical equations.

First Equation of Motion (Velocity-Time Relation): This equation calculates the final velocity ({tex}v{/tex}) of an object based on its initial velocity ({tex}u{/tex}), constant acceleration ({tex}a{/tex}), and time ({tex}t{/tex}):

{tex}v = u + at{/tex}

Second Equation of Motion (Position-Time Relation): This equation calculates the displacement ({tex}s{/tex}) of an object based on its initial velocity ({tex}u{/tex}), time ({tex}t{/tex}), and constant acceleration ({tex}a{/tex}):

{tex}s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2{/tex}

Third Equation of Motion (Position-Velocity Relation): This equation relates the final velocity ({tex}v{/tex}), initial velocity ({tex}u{/tex}), acceleration ({tex}a{/tex}), and displacement ({tex}s{/tex}) without requiring the time interval:

{tex}v^2 = u^2 + 2as{/tex}

Application of Kinematic Equations: The three equations of motion ({tex}v = u + at{/tex}, {tex}s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2{/tex}, and {tex}v^2 = u^2 + 2as{/tex}) are used to solve real-world problems where an object moves with a steady change in velocity. Common examples include a vehicle accelerating at a constant rate on a highway or an object in "free fall," where it drops vertically under the constant pull of Earth's gravity ({tex}g \approx 9.8{/tex} m/s²).

Sign Convention in Kinematics: To ensure mathematical accuracy when using motion equations, a specific direction must be defined as positive (e.g., upward or to the right). Any movement, velocity, or acceleration occurring in the opposite direction, as well as slowing down (retardation), must be represented using negative values in the calculations.

Limitations of Kinematic Equations: The three standard equations of motion ({tex}v = u + at{/tex}, {tex}s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2{/tex}, and {tex}v^2 = u^2 + 2as{/tex}) are strictly valid only under two conditions: the object must be moving in a single straight line, and the acceleration must remain constant throughout the entire time interval being measured.

The average speed of an object is the total distance travelled divided by the time interval during which this distance is covered.

The average velocity of an object in a time interval is the change in position (also known as displacement) divided by the time interval in which the change in position (or displacement) occurs.

The average acceleration of an object over a time interval is the change in its velocity divided by the time interval.

For the motion of an object in a straight line with constant acceleration, the five physical quantities — displacement (s), time interval (t), initial velocity (u), final velocity (v) and acceleration (a), can be related by the following set of kinematic equations,

{tex}v = u + at{/tex}

{tex}s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2{/tex}

{tex}v^2 = u^2 + 2as{/tex}

sections.label: Motion in a Plane
sections.html:
Two-dimensional motion:
Two-Dimensional Motion occurs when an object moves simultaneously along two directions, usually represented by x and y axes. Examples include a projectile or a turning vehicle. Such motion cannot be described using a single straight line and requires coordinate-based representation.

Circular Motion:
Circular motion takes place when an object moves along a circular path with a fixed radius. The distance travelled is along the circumference, while displacement is the shortest straight-line distance between initial and final positions, making them generally unequal.

Uniform circular motion:
Uniform circular motion occurs when an object moves in a circular path with constant speed. Although speed remains unchanged, the direction of motion continuously changes, resulting in a change in velocity. Hence, acceleration exists even without any change in speed.

Velocity in Circular Motion:
In circular motion, velocity at any point is always directed along the tangent to the circle at that point. As the object moves, this direction keeps changing continuously, which means velocity is not constant despite constant speed.

Acceleration in circular motion:
Acceleration in circular motion arises due to continuous change in direction of velocity. This acceleration is always directed towards the center of the circle and is called centripetal acceleration. It exists even when the speed of the object remains constant.
{tex}a=\frac{v^2}{r}{/tex}

Distance and Displacement in Circular Motion:
In circular motion, distance is measured along the curved path, while displacement is the straight-line separation between initial and final positions. After one complete revolution, the distance equals the circumference, but displacement becomes zero as the object returns to its starting point.

When an object moves in a circular path with constant (uniform) speed, its motion is called the uniform circular motion.

sections.label: What is a Mixture?
sections.html:
A mixture which has a uniform composition throughout is called a homogeneous mixture or solution. A mixture which contains physically distinct parts and has a non-uniform composition is called a heterogeneous mixture.

Pure substances are elements or compounds. Pure substances are made up of only one kind of entity. Pure substances have a fixed composition.

Types of Mixtures
Mixtures can also be grouped on the basis of their physical states:

  SOLID LIQUID GAS
SOLID Salt and sugar Salt and water Dust in air
LIQUID Mercury and copper Alcohol and water Clouds
GAS Hydrogen and palladium Oxygen and water Air
PURE SUBSTANCE MIXTURE
Pure substance consists of a single type of substance. Mixture consists of two or more pure substances.
Pure substance cannot be separated into other substances by physical methods. Mixture can be separated into its components by physical methods.
Pure substance has its own definite properties. Mixture shows the properties of its components.

Elements are made up of one kind of atoms only. Compounds are made up of one kind of molecules.

Types of mixture on the basis of miscibility:

Homogeneous Mixture Heterogeneous Mixture
It consists of single phase. It consists of two or more phases.
Uniform composition Does not have uniform composition
Example: Sugar dissolved in water Example: Air, sand and common salt.

A substance is a kind of matter that cannot be separated into other kinds of matter by any physical process. A pure substance is made up of the same kind of element.

The compound is a pure substance made up of two or more elements combined chemically in a definite ratio.
Characteristics of compound:

  • The properties of a compound differ from those of its constituents.
  • The compound has a fixed melting point and boiling point.
  • A Compound is a homogeneous substance.
  • Constituent elements can be separated by the chemical process.

Mixtures is made up of two or more elements or compounds mixed in any ratio/proportion.
Properties of mixture:

  • It may be homogeneous or heterogeneous.
  • The properties of constituent substances are retained.
  • No new compound is formed.

A substance is said to undergo a physical change when only the physical properties such as the shape, size, colour or state of the substance change. No new substance is formed. A substance is said to undergo a chemical change when a new substance with completely new properties (physical and chemical) is formed.

sections.label: What is a Solution?
sections.html:
A solution is a homogeneous mixture of two or more substances. The component of the solution that dissolves the other component in it is called the solvent. The component of the solution that is dissolved in the solvent is called the solute.

Properties of a solution

  • A solution is a homogeneous mixture.
  • It cannot be seen by naked eyes.
  • Because of very small particle size, they do not scatter a beam of light passing through the solution. So, the path of light is not visible in a solution.
  • The solute particles cannot be separated from the mixture by the  process of filtration.

Alloys: Alloys are mixtures of two or more metals or a metal and a non-metal and cannot be separated into their components by physical methods. Brass is a mixture of approximately 30% zinc and 70% copper.

Mass by mass percentage of a solution {tex}=\frac{\text { Mass of solute }}{\text { Mass of solution }} \times 100{/tex}
Mass by volume percentage of a solution {tex}=\frac{\text { Mass of solute }}{\text { Mass of solution }} \times 100{/tex}

Suspension: A suspension is a heterogeneous mixture in which the solute particles do not dissolve but remain suspended throughout the bulk of the medium.
Properties of a Suspension:

  • Suspension is a heterogeneous mixture.
  • The particles of a suspension can be seen by the naked eye.
  • The particles of a suspension scatter a beam of light passing through it and make its path visible.

The amount of solute that has dissolved in a specific amount of solvent or solution is measured as solution concentration.

Unsaturated solutions are those that contain less solute than the maximum that can be dissolved. A saturated solution is one that contains the maximum quantity of solute that can be dissolved.

Solubility: The amount of a solute that dissolves in a solvent is known as its solubility.
Factors Affecting Solubility:

  • Temperature: Solubility increases with temperature. The situation is different for gases. With the increase in temperature, they became less soluble in each other and in water, but more soluble in organic solvents.
  • Pressure: For the majority of solid and liquid solutes, pressure does not affect solubility. The solubility of gas is directly proportional to the pressure of this gas.

Suspension: A suspension is a heterogeneous mixture in which the solute particles do not dissolve but remain suspended throughout the bulk of the medium.

Colloidal Solutions: A colloidal solution is a mixture in which the substances are regularly suspended in a fluid. Colloidal can be Classified as Foam, Emulsion, Sol etc.

Tyndall effect : Tyndall effect is the scattering of light by particles in a colloid or else particles in a very fine suspension

Properties of a colloid

  • A colloid is a heterogeneous mixture.
  • The size of particles of a colloid is too small to be individually seen by naked eyes.
  • Colloids are big enough to scatter a beam of light passing through it and make its path visible.
  • They do not settle down when left undisturbed, that is, a colloid is quite stable

sections.label: Separating the Components of a Mixture
sections.html:
Evaporation: Out of two components of a mixture one can evaporate and other has higher boil point. For example of mixture of dye and water. Out of water and dye water evaporates but dye is left behind in petri dish.

Centrifugation: Centrifugation uses centrifugal force for the separation of two liquids in a mixture.

Applications of centrifugation:

  • Used for blood and urine tests in diagnostic facilities.
  • Used to separate butter from cream in dairies and at home.
  • Utilised in washing machines to extract water from drenched clothing.

Sublimation: Sublimation is the transition of a substance from solid phase to gaseous phase without changing into liquid phase. Separation of ammonium chloride and salt by sublimation:

Separation of immiscible liquids by using separating funnel. Water from oil can be separated by first opening the stop cock till water is removed in one beaker, then after wards oil can be

Chromatography is used to separate the different components in a liquid mixture.
Applications of Chromatography:

  • To separate
  • colours in a dye
  • pigments from natural colours
  • drugs from blood

Paper Chromatography

Distillation is a method for separating the component substances from a liquid mixture by selective evaporation and condensation. Uses of distillation are Production of gasoline, distilled water, xylene, alcohol, paraffin, kerosene etc.

Fractional distillation is the process of separation of chemical compounds by their boiling point.

Air is a homogeneous mixture and can be separated into its components by fractional distillation.

Crystallization: Crystallisation is a separation technique in which solids are separated from a solution.
Applications of Crystallization:

  • Purification of salt that we get from sea water.
  •  Separation of crystals of alum (phitkari) from impure samples

Water purification system in water works

sections.label: Balanced and Unbalanced Forces
sections.html:
Force: It is the force that enables us to do any work. To do anything, either we pull or push the object. Therefore, pull or push is called force.
Example, to open a door, either we push or pull it. A drawer is pulled to open and pushed to close.

Effect of Force

  1. Force can move a stationary body or object.
  2. Force can stop a moving body.
  3. Force can change the direction of a moving object.
  4. Force can change the speed of moving body.
  5. Force can change the shape and size of an object.

If the resultant of all the forces acting on a body is zero, then the forces are called balanced forces. Balanced forces do not change the state of rest or motion of an object.

If the resultant of all the forces acting on a body is not zero, then the forces are called unbalanced forces. Unbalanced forces can move a stationary body or can stop it. Suppose a toy car is lying on the ground and we push it with our hand, it starts moving because the opposing force of friction is very less.

Unbalanced forces can do the following:

  • Move a stationary object
  • Increase the speed of a moving object
  • Decrease the speed of a moving object
  • Stop a moving object
  • Change the shape and size of an object

Force of friction: It is a force extended when two surfaces are in contact with each other. It always acts in a direction opposite to the direction of motion of the object.

sections.label: First Law of Motion
sections.html:
Galileo first of all said that object move with constant speed when no force act on them. But practically it is not possible for any object.

Newton has given three laws to describe the motion of the bodies. These are known as Newton’s laws of motion.

Newton’s first law of motion states that a body at rest will remain at rest, and a body in motion will continue in motion in a straight line with a uniform speed, unless it is compelled by an external force to change its state of rest or of uniform motion.

The tendency of objects to remain in the state of rest or to keep moving uniformly is called Inertia.  This is why the first law of motion is also known as the law of inertia.
Example: We fall back when a vehicle starts moving in the forward direction because our body is in the rest state and it opposes the motion of the vehicle.

sections.label: Inertia and Mass
sections.html:
The inertia of an object is dependent upon its mass.

Lighter objects have less inertia, that is, they can easily change their state of rest or motion.

Heavier objects have large inertia and therefore they show more resistance. Hence ‘Mass’ is called a measure of the inertia of an object.

A body at rest continues to be at rest and a body in motion continues to be in motion. This property of a body is called its inertia. The inertia of a body is measured by the magnitude of force required to change the state of the body. The force required to change the state of a heavier body is more than the force required to change the state of the lighter body. This is because the mass of the heavier body is more than the mass of the lighter body. So ‘The mass of a body is a measure of its inertia.’

sections.label: Second Law of Motion
sections.html:
Molecular mass of an element is defined as the sum of the masses of the elements present in the molecule. Molecular mass is obtained by multiplying the atomic mass of an element with the number of atoms in the molecule and then adding the masses of all the elements in the molecule.

The second law of motion states that the rate of change of momentum of an object is proportional to the applied unbalanced force in the direction of force.

The quantity of motion in a body depends on the product of mass and velocity of the body termed as momentum.

The momentum (p) of a body is defined as the product of its mass (m) and velocity (v) and has the same direction as that of the velocity. Its SI unit is kilogram metre per second
(kg m s-1).Momentum = mass {tex}\times{/tex} velocity
p = m {tex}\times{/tex} v

The momentum of a stationary object -
Let the mass of a stationary object be ‘m’,
Let the velocity of a stationary object be ‘v’,
The stationary object has no velocity, so v = 0,
Therefore, p = m {tex}\times{/tex} v = m {tex}\times{/tex} 0 = 0
So, the momentum of a stationary object is zero.

Momentum has both magnitude as well as direction; hence it is a vector quantity. Momentum of a body at rest is zero.

We know that the velocity of an object can be changed by applying an unbalanced force to it. Similarly, the momentum of an object can change by applying an unbalanced force.

Mathematical Formulation of the Second Law of Motion

Change in momentum {tex}\propto{/tex} p2 - p1
{tex}\propto{/tex} mv - mu
{tex}\propto{/tex} m(v - u)
Rate of change of momentum {tex}\propto \frac{m(v-u)}t{/tex}
Force {tex}\propto \frac{m(v-u)}t{/tex}
Force {tex}= k \frac{m(v-u)} t{/tex}
Force = k ma
Force = ma		 Initial velocity = u
Final velocity = v
Acceleration {tex}=\frac{(v-u)}t{/tex}
1 unit of force = k {tex}\times{/tex} (1 kg) {tex}\times{/tex} (1 ms-2)
K = 1

When an acceleration of 1 m/s2 is seen in a body of mass 1 kg, then the force applied on the body is said to be 1 Newton.

The first law of motion can be mathematically stated from the mathematical expression for the second law of motion.
F = ma
or
{tex}F = \frac{m(v - u)}t{/tex}
or
Ft = mv - mu

sections.label: Third Law of Motion
sections.html:
Two forces acting from opposite directions are called Action and Reaction Forces.

Newton’s third law of motion states that: To every action there is an equal and opposite reaction and they act on two different bodies.

Example of third law of motion:

  1. When a gun is fired, it exerts a forward force on the bullet. The bullet exerts an equal and opposite reaction force on the gun. This results in the recoil of the gun. Since the gun has a much greater mass than the bullet, the acceleration of the gun is much less than the acceleration of the bullet.
  2. As the sailor jumps forward out of a rowing boat, the force on the boat moves it backwards.

sections.label: Conservation of Momentum
sections.html:
According to the law of conservation of momentum, the sum of momenta of two objects before collision is equal to the sum of momenta after collision, provided there is no external unbalanced force acting on the objects.


Two balls A and B having certain initial velocities collide with each other. Conditions before the collision-

  • There is no unbalanced force acting upon them
  • The initial velocity of A is greater than the initial velocity of B

Facts about Conservation Laws

  • They are considered the fundamental laws in physics.
  • They are based on observations and experiments.
  • They cannot be proved but can be verified or disproved with the help of experiments.
  • A single experiment is enough to disprove a law, while a single experiment is not enough to prove the same.
  • It requires a large number of experiments to prove the law.
The momentum of ball A before collision = mAuA
The momentum of ball A after collision = mAvA
The momentum of ball B before collision = mBuB
The momentum of ball B after collision = mBuB
Rate of change of momentum of ball A = {tex}\frac{m_A(v_A-u_A)}{t}{/tex} =Force of action FAB
Rate of change of momentum of ball B = {tex}\frac{m_B(v_B-u_B)}{t}{/tex} = Force of reaction FBA		 According to third law of motion
FAB = -FBA [-ve sign shows opposite force]
{tex}\frac{m_A(v_A-u_A)}{t}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{m_B(v_B-u_B)}{t}{/tex}
mAvA - mAuA = -mBvB + mBuB
mAvA + mBvB = mAuA + mBuB
or
mAuA + mBuB = mAvA + mBvB
Momentum before collision = Momentum after collision

sections.label: Work
sections.html:
Work is defined as the product of the force applied on an object and displacement caused due to the applied force in the direction of the force. Work is a scalar quantity. It has no direction of its own but a magnitude.

When you play a certain force ‘F Newton’ on an object and the object moves a distance of ‘ d meters’ in the direction where you applied the force then, the amount of work done can be calculated as:
Work done = Force {tex}\times{/tex} Displacement
W = F {tex}\times{/tex} d

The unit of work is Newton metre (Nm).
When a force of 1 Newton moves a body through a distance of 1 metre in its own direction, then the work done is known as 1 joule.
1 joule = 1 Newton {tex}\times{/tex} 1 metre
1 J = 1 Nm
Thus, the SI unit of work is joule.

CASE -1:  If the force is acting in the direction of displacement
Let a block placed at a point P and acted upon by a constant force F and the block is displaced through a distance s to a new position Q. Then,
W = Fx

CASE 2: IF THE FORCE IS ACTING IN THE DIRECTION OPPOSITE TO THE DISPLACEMENT
In this case, the work done is Negative, i.e.

CASE 3: IF THE FORCE IS ACTING PERPENDICULAR TO THE DISPLACEMENT
In this case, there is no displacement in the direction or parallel to the force.
Work done in this case is zero. i.e.
Therefore -
W = F * s = F * 0 = 0 (because the displacement is zero)

CASE 4: IF THE FORCE IS CAUSING NO DISPLACEMENT-
In this case displacement is zero, therefore
W = F s = 0

The work done by a force can be either positive or negative.

  1. Work done is negative when the force acts opposite to the direction of displacement.
  2. Work done is positive when the force is in the direction of displacement.
  3. Work done is zero when a force acts at right angles to the direction of motion of the body.

1 Kilo joule (1 kJ) = 1000 J
1 mega joule (1 MJ) = 106 J

sections.label: Energy
sections.html:
Energy is the ability to do work. The amount of energy possessed by a body is equal to the amount of work it can do when its energy is released. Energy is a scalar quantity.

The SI unit of energy is joule. The energy required to do 1 joule of work is called 1 joule energy. Joule is a small unit of energy, a bigger unit of energy called kilojoule is also used. Its symbol is kJ.
1 kilojoule = 1000 joules
1 kJ = 1000 J

Energy exists in many forms. The main forms of energy are:

  1. Kinetic energy
  2. Potential energy
  3. Chemical energy
  4. Heat energy
  5. Light energy
  6. Sound energy
  7. Electrical energy
  8. Nuclear energy

The energy of a body due to its motion is called kinetic energy. Example a moving water can do work by turning the blades of a turbine for generating electricity.

Examples of kinetic energy

  1. A ball rolling on a surface because it can set another ball into motion by striking it.
  2. A bullet fired from a gun as it is able to penetrate some distance into a target which it strikes.
  3. Water in motion as it can turn a wheel or a turbine.
  4. A moving hammer etc.

Formula for Kinetic Energy
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Work done {tex}\rightarrow{/tex}  W = F {tex}\times{/tex} s ...(i)
due to force the velocity changes to v, and the acceleration produced is a
{tex}\therefore{/tex} relationship between v, u, a and s = v2 - u2 = 2as
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}s =\frac{v^{2}-u^{2}}{2 a}{/tex} ...(ii)
F = ma ...(iii)
Substitute (ii) and (iii) in (i) we get
W = F {tex}\times{/tex} s
{tex}= ma \times \frac{v^{2}-u^{2}}{2 a}{/tex}
W = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} m (v2 - u2)
If u = 0, (object starts at rest)
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{W}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex}
Work done = Change in kinetic energy
{tex}\therefore E_{k}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex}

The energy possessed by a body because of its position or configuration is known as potential energy.

Potential Energy of An Object At A Height:
Suppose a body of mass m is raised to a height h above the surface of the earth. The force acting on the body is the gravitational pull of the earth m x g which acts in the downward direction. To lift  a body above the surface of the earth, we have to do work against this force of gravity.
Work done = Force x Distance
So, W = m {tex}\times{/tex} g {tex}\times{/tex} h
This work gets stored up in the body as potential energy.
Thus, potential energy = m {tex}\times{/tex} g {tex}\times{/tex} h
Where m is the mass of the body, g is acceleration due to gravity; h is the height of the body above the reference point.

Law of Conservation of Energy: According to the law of conservation of energy, energy can only be converted from one form to another; it can neither be created or destroyed. The total energy before and after the transformation remains the same.

Mechanical Energy: It is the sum of kinetic and potential energy of an object. Therefore, it is the energy obtained by an object due to motion or by the virtue of its location. Example, a bicycle climbing a hill possesses kinetic energy as well as potential energy.

Heat Energy: It is the energy obtained by an object due to its temperature. It is also called Thermal Energy. Example- energy possessed by a hot cup.

Chemical Energy: It is the energy accumulated in the bonds of chemical compounds. Chemical energy is released at the time of chemical reactions. Example, energy possessed by natural gas and biomass.

Ionization Energy: It is the energy that binds electrons with its nucleus.

Sonic Energy: It is the energy produced by a substance as it vibrates. This energy flows through the substance in the form of sound waves. Example, music instruments produce sound energy.

Nuclear Energy: It is the energy present in the nucleus of an atom. Nuclear energy releases when the nucleus combines or separate. Therefore, we can say that every atom in this universe comprises of nucleus energy. Example, uranium is a radioactive metal capable of producing nuclear energy in nuclear power plants.

Light Energy: It is the energy due to light or electromagnetic waves. It is also called as Radiant Energy or Electromagnetic Energy. Example, energy from the sun.

Electrical Energy: It is kind of kinetic energy caused due to the motion of electrons. It depends upon the speed of electrons. As the speed increases so does the electrical energy.
Example, electricity produced by a battery, lightning at thunderstorms.

sections.label: The Structure of an Atom
sections.html:
Thomson’s Model of an Atom

  • According to J.J. Thomson, the structure of an atom can be compared to Christmas pudding where electrons are present inside a positive sphere.
  • An atom is composed of a positively charged sphere in which electrons are embedded.
  • Atom is neutral as the positive and negative charged are equal in proportion.

Rutherford's atomic model

  • Most of alpha particles passed through gold foil undeflected.
  • Some of the alpha particles deflected by foil by small angles.
  • One out of every 12000 particles appeared to rebound.

Rutherford concluded the model of the atom from the α-particle scattering experiment as:

  • There is a positively charged centre in an atom called the nucleus. Nearly all the mass of an atom resides in the nucleus.
  • The electrons revolve around the nucleus in well-defined orbits.
  • The size of the nucleus is very small as compared to the size of the atom.

Drawbacks of Rutherford’s model

  • He explained that the electrons in an atom revolve around the nucleus in well-defined orbits. Particles in a circular orbit would experience acceleration.
  • Thus, the revolving electron would lose energy and finally fall into the nucleus.
  • But this cannot take place as the atom would be unstable and matter would not exist in the form we know.

Radioactivity is the process by which an unstable nucleus of an atom loses energy by giving out particles. This process is spontaneous. It does so by giving out particles such as alpha and beta particles.

Assumptions of Neil Bohr model

  • Only certain special orbits known as discrete orbits of electrons are allowed inside the atom.
  • While revolving in discrete orbits, the electrons do not radiate energy.
  • Energy is emitted or absorbed by an atom only when an electrons moves from one orbit to another.

sections.label: How are Electrons Distributed In Different Orbits (Shells)?
sections.html:
Bohr-Bury Scheme

  • The filling of electrons in an atom is done in accordance with '2n2', where 'n' is the number of shells.
  • The outermost shell can't hold more than 8 electrons, while the second last shell can't have more than 18 electrons.
  • The outermost shell can't hold more than 2 electrons and the penultimate shell can't hold more than 8 electrons unless the preceding inner shell is filled completely obeying '2n2'.

 

The maximum number of electrons present in a shell is given by the formula 2n2, where ‘n’ is the orbit number or energy level index, 1, 2, 3, ….

The maximum number of electrons in different shells are as follows:

The first orbit will have 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 2

The second orbit will have 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 8,

The third orbit will have 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 32 = 18,

The fourth orbit 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 42 = 32 and so on.

Examples:

  1. K19 = 2, 8, 8, 1
  2. Al13 = 2, 8, 3
  3. F9 = 2, 7
  4. Ne10 = 2, 8
  5. Na11 = 2, 8, 1

sections.label: Atomic Number and Mass Number
sections.html:
The total number of proton lying in the nucleus of any atom is called the atomic number. It is denoted by 'Z'. For neutral atom, number of protons and electrons are equal.

Mass number is the sum of the total number of protons and neutrons lying in the nucleus of an atom. The mass number is denoted by 'A'.

Atomic Symbol Notation

Atomic Number (Z) = Number of protons in an atom
Mass number = Number of proton + Number of Neutron

sections.label: Isotopes
sections.html:
Isotopes are defined as the atoms of the same element, having the same atomic number (number of protons) but different mass numbers (number of protons + neutrons).

Isotopes of Hydrogen:

Use Isotopes:

  • The fuel of Nuclear Reactor - Isotope of Uranium
  • Treatment of Cancer - Isotope of Cobalt
  • Treatment of Goiter - Isotope of Iodine

Atoms of different elements with different atomic numbers, which have the same mass number, are known as isobars.
Examples of isobars: Calcium and Argon: both have the same mass number - 40
20Ca40 and 18Ar40

Isobars Atomic Number (Z) Mass number (A) Electrons (e) Protons (p) Neutrons (n)
{tex}{}_{18}^{40}Ar{/tex} 18 40 18 18 22 (40-18)
{tex}{}_{19}^{40}Ar{/tex} 19 40 19 19 21 (40-19)
{tex}{}_{20}^{40}Ar{/tex} 20 40 20 20 20 (40-20)

sections.label: Laws of Chemical Combination
sections.html:
Law of chemical combination: The chemical reaction between two or more substance giving rise to products is governed by certain laws. This laws are called Laws of Chemical Combination.
Law of Chemical Combination

  • Law of conservation of Mass
  • Law of Constant Proportion
  • Law of Multiple Proportion
  • Law of Reciprocal Proportion
  • Gay Lussac's law of combining volume

Law of conservation of mass: Mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.
e.g., A + B {tex}\to{/tex} C + D
Reactants {tex}\to{/tex} Products
Mass of reactants = Mass of products

Law of constant proportion: In a chemical substance the elements are always present in definite proportions by mass.
E.g., In water, the ratio of the mass of hydrogen to the mass of oxygen is always 1 : 8 respectively.

Dalton picked up the idea of divisibility of matter, which was till then just a philosophy. According to Dalton’s atomic theory:  All matter, whether an element, a compound or a mixture is composed of small particles called atoms.

The postulates of Dalton’s atomic theory:

  • All matter is made of very tiny particles called atoms.
  • Atoms are indivisible particles, which cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction.
  • Atoms of a given element are identical in mass and chemical properties.
  • Atoms of different elements have different masses and chemical properties.
  • Atoms combine in the ratio of small whole numbers to form compounds.
  • The relative number and kinds of atoms are constant in a given compound.

Chemical Reactions: In a chemical reaction, two or more molecules interact to produce new compounds and are called reactants, whereas the newly formed compounds are called products. In a chemical reaction, a chemical change must occur, which is generally observed with physical changes like precipitation, heat production, colour change, etc.

sections.label: What is an Atom?
sections.html:
An atom is the defining structure of an element, which cannot be broken by any chemical means. Atomic radius is measured in nanometres.

  • 1/109 m = 1 nm
  • 1 m = 109 nm

Symbols for some elements as proposed by Dalton:

IUPAC: International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry.

The atomic symbol has three parts:-

  • The symbol X: the usual element symbol
  • The atomic number A: equal to the number of protons
  • The mass number Z: equal to the total number of protons and neutrons in an element.

Atomic Radius is the distance between an atom’s nucleus and outer electron shell.The atomic radius is calculated by measuring the distance between the nuclei of two identical atoms bonded together. Half this distance is the atomic radius.

Atomic mass is the total of the masses of the electrons, neutrons, and protons in an atom, or in a group of atoms, the average mass. Atomic mass is commonly expressed as per the international agreement in terms of a unified atomic mass unit (AMU).

Atomic mass unit: 1/12 of the mass of a carbon-12 atom in its ground state

Atomic masses of a few elements

Element Atomic Mass(u)
Hydrogen 1
Carbon 12
Nitrogen 14
Oxygen 16
Sodium 23
Magnesium 24
Sulphur 32
Chlorine 35.5
Calcium 40

sections.label: What is a Molecule?
sections.html:
The smallest identifiable unit into which a pure substance may be divided while retaining its composition and chemical properties is a molecule, which is a collection of two or more atoms.

Molecules of elements: A molecule is a collection of two or more chemically bound atoms, whether they are from the same element or another. Example of  molecules of elements are when two hydrogen (H2) and one oxygen (O2) atoms interact, one water molecule is created.

Types of molecules and its example

Molecules of some compounds

Compound Combining Elements
Water (H2O) Hydrogen, Oxygen
Ammonia (NH3) Nitrogen, Hydrogen
Carbon Dioxide (CO2) Carbon, Oxygen
Hydrogen Chloride (HCl) Hydrogen, Chlorine
Methane (CH4) Carbon, Hydrogen
Ethane (C2H6) Carbon, Hydrogen
Sodium Chloride (NaCl) Sodium, Chlorine
Copper Oxide (CuO) Copper and Oxygen

The number of atoms constituting a molecule is known as its atomicity.

sections.label: Writing Chemical Formulae
sections.html:
Chemical formulae is the symbolic representation of the compounds of a compound.

Compounds are substances consisting of two or more different types of elements in a fixed ratio of its atoms.

An ion is an atom or molecule which has gained or lost one or more of its valence electrons, giving it a net positive or negative charge. 

A negatively charged particle is called an anion, and a positively charged particle is called a cation.

Ionic compounds are chemical compounds in which ions are held together by specialised bonds called ionic bonds. An Ionic compound always contains an equal amount of positive and negative charge.

How to write a Formula of a Compound

  • Write the symbols of the corresponding elements of the compound as explained above
  • Write the valencies of the elements of the compound
  • Crossover the valencies of the elements

Characteristics of Chemical formula

  • The valencies or charges on ion must balance
  • When a compound is formed of metal and non-metal, a symbol of metal comes first e.g. CaO, NaCl, CuO
  • When polyatomic ions are used, the ions are enclosed in brackets before writing the number to show the ratio. E.g. Ca(OH)2, (NH4)2SOs

Formula of hydrogen sulphide

Formula of hydrogen chloride

Formula of the compound would be HCl

Formula for aluminium oxide:

sections.label: Mole Concept
sections.html:
The molar mass of a substance is the mass of 1 mole of that substance. It is equal to the 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms of that element.

In a substance, the amount of entities present for e.g. atoms, molecules, ions, is defined as a mole. Mole concept is the most convenient ways of expressing the amount of reactants and product in the reaction.

The molar mass/molecular weight is actually the sum of the total mass in grams of the atoms present to make up a molecule per mole. The unit of molar mass is grams/mole.

The molecular mass of HNO3 can be calculated as:
Atomic mass of H = 1u
Atomic mass of N = 14u
Atomic mass of O = 16u
Molecular mass of HNO3 = 1 + 14 + (16 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) = 63u

The number of moles in a given sample of an element/compound can be calculated by dividing the total mass of the sample by the molar mass of the element/compound, as described by the following formula
Number of Moles {tex}= \frac{Mass \; of \; the \; Sample}{Molar \; Mass}{/tex}

The total number of atoms/molecules in a sample can be calculated by multiplying the number of moles with the Avogadro constant. This formula can be written as:
The total number of atoms/molecules = Number of moles {tex}\times{/tex} Avogadro’s constant

sections.label: Production of Sound
sections.html:
Sound is produced when object vibrates or sound is produced by vibrating objects.

Sound: Sound is a form of energy that makes us hear. It travels in the form of waves.

A sound is produced when an object vibrates, that is they move in a ‘to-and-fro’ motion.
For instance,

  • When we strike a tuning fork or a stretched rubber band, it vibrates and produces sound.
  • The human voice is produced because of the vibration of the vocal cords.
  • String instruments produce sound as their strings vibrate.
  • When a bird flaps its wings a sound is produced.
  • A flute produces sound because the air column of the flute vibrates as air passes through it.

Sound can be produced by the following methods:

  • By vibrating string (sitar)
  • By vibrating air(flute)
  • By vibrating membrane (table, drum)
  • By friction in objects.
  • By scratching or scrubbing the objects etc.

sections.label: Propagation of Sound
sections.html:
Loudness: It is how our ears respond to a sound. Two sounds with same intensity can vary in loudness only because we can detect one sound easier than the other.

The amount of sound energy that passes through a unit area per second is called its intensity.

Noise: It is an unpleasant sound.


 

Note: A sound with more than one frequency is called a Note. It is pleasant to listen.

The sound which has single frequency throughout is called a Tone.

The timbre or quality of sound is a characteristic with which we can differentiate between different sounds even if they have same pitch and amplitude.

Pitch of a sound depends upon:

  1. the frequency of the sound
  2. size of the object producing the sound
  3. type of the object producing the sound
